---
title: "Lesson 7: Data Cleaning"
subtitle: "DS 01-101: In-Depth Introduction to Data Science (Spring 2026)"
author: "Hung Tong"
institute: "Rowan University"
editor: visual
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
echo: true
eval: false
code-overflow: wrap
format: 
  html:
    self-contained: false
---

## Readings

From **R for Data Science** by Drs. Hadley Wickham, Garrett Grolemund, and Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel:

-   [Missing Values](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/missing-values.html)

## Topics

-   Data cleaning
-   Duplicate observations
-   Missing values
-   Inconsistent formats

## Data cleaning

![](https://github.com/hungtong/DS-01-101/blob/main/image/7_dirty_Data.png?raw=true){fig-align="center" width="382"}

-   **Data cleaning** refers to the process of preparing "dirty" or "messy" data for further exploration and modeling. It is often the most time-consuming step in a data science project.

-   Although data cleaning is open-ended in nature, it generally involves handling the following issues:

    -   Duplicate observations

    -   Outliers due to errors

    -   Missing values

    -   Inconsistent formats

## Duplicate observations

-   Duplicate observations can lead to biased results, so they should be identified and discarded.

```{r}

df <- data.frame(
  FirstName = c('Marco', 'Sharon', 'Martin', 'Marco', 'Tanya', 'Michael'),
  LastName = c('Bray', 'Turner', 'Lewis', 'Bray', 'Jones', 'Watson'),
  Age = c(45, 38, 55, 45, 28, 60),
  BirthPlace = c('New York', 'Chicago', 'Los Angeles', 'New York', 'Boston', 'Houston')
)
```

-   The `duplicated()` function in base R can be used to identify observations that already appear earlier.

```{r}

# for a vector, TRUE if an observation is duplicated
duplicated(df$FirstName)   

# for a data frame, TRUE if a row is duplicated
duplicated(df)   

# return the duplicated observations
df[duplicated(df), ]

# return the non-duplicated (unique) observations
df[!duplicated(df), ] 
```

-   The `distinct()` function in `dplyr` is also a convenient function to discard duplicate observations.

```{r}
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)

df |> distinct()
```

## Outliers due to errors

-   Outliers can be extreme but valid values but they can also be due to errors. In the latter case, we want to identify and remove them.

-   Consider data on sale price in millions of dollars and size in thousands of sq. ft. of some properties.

```{r}
#| eval: true

properties <- data.frame(
  Size = c(2166, 751, 5, 2422, 224, 3917, 2866, -451, 1698, 1046, 1108, 405),
  Price = c(  10.6, 2.6, 12, 30.5, 1.8, 20.0,  8.0, 12.5, 10.0,  6.7,  5.8, 4.5)
)
```

-   The data set has some unusual small properties. To remove them, we can do the following:

```{r}
#| eval: true

properties <- properties[properties >= 100, ]
```

## Missing values

-   Missing values refer to data that was not recorded, unavailable, or unknown during data collection or entry. In R, missing values are represented as `NA`.

-   There are two common approaches to handling missing values:

    -   **Listwise deletion** removes observations with missing values.

    -   **Imputation** replaces missing values with new values. For example, mean imputation replaces missing values in a column by the mean of observed values in that column.

-   The `pima` data set, originated from the National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases, consists of 768 female patients of Pima Indian heritage, all aged at least $21$ and living near Phoenix, Arizona, USA. Among these patients, 268 were diagnosed with diabetes. Several medical variables were measured for these patients.

![From Şafak Selim Sofioğlu](https://github.com/hungtong/DS-01-101/blob/main/image/7_pima.png?raw=true){fig-align="center" width="386"}

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)

load(url('https://github.com/hungtong/DS-01-101/raw/refs/heads/main/dataset/pima.rdata'))

pima <- as_tibble(pima)
glimpse(pima)
```

-   `is.na()` is a useful function to identify missing values in a vector.

```{r}

# for a vector, TRUE if an observation is a missing value
is.na(pima$Insulin)   

# number of missing values for Insulin
sum(is.na(pima$Insulin))   

# number of observed values for Insulin
sum(!is.na(pima$Insulin))

# all observed values in Insulin
pima$Insulin[!is.na(pima$Insulin)]  

# mean of all observed values in Insulin
mean(pima$Insulin[!is.na(pima$Insulin)])   
```

-   `complete.cases()` allows us to quickly identify observations without missing values in a data frame.

```{r}

# for a data frame, TRUE if a row has NO missing value
complete.cases(pima)  

# number of rows WITHOUT missing values
sum(complete.cases(pima)) 

# return rows WITHOUT missing values
pima[complete.cases(pima), ]  
```

-   If our interest is to identify missing values, we can pair `complete.cases()` with the not `!` operator.

```{r}

# TRUE if a row DOES HAVE a missing value
!complete.cases(pima)

# number of rows WITH missing values
sum(!complete.cases(pima))

# return rows WITH missing values
pima[!complete.cases(pima), ]
```

-   `na.omit()` removes rows with missing values.

```{r}

na.omit(pima)
```

-   `drop_na()` is a more flexible function to remove rows with missing values

```{r}

# remove all rows with missing values
pima |> drop_na()

# remove only rows with missing values on BMI
pima |> drop_na(BMI)

# remove only rows with missing values on BMI and SkinThickness
pima |> drop_na(BMI, SkinThickness)
```

-   Mean imputation can be done as follow

```{r}

# method 1
pima$Insulin[is.na(pima$Insulin)] <- mean(pima$Insulin[!is.na(pima$Insulin)]) 

# method 2
pima$Insulin[is.na(pima$Insulin)] <- mean(pima$Insulin, na.rm = TRUE)
```

## Inconsistent Format

-   Inconsistent formats commonly occur with categorical and date/time variables.

-   The `tips` data set contains information about restaurant bills, tips, and customers. Each observation represents a single dining party.

-   `tota_bill` - Total bill amount (in dollars) for the meal, before the tip

-   `tip` - Tip amount (in dollars) left by the customer

-   `sex` - Gender of the person paying the bill.

-   `day` - Day of the week when the meal occurred.

-   `time`- Time of day the meal took place: `1` for lunch and `2` for dinner

-   `size` - Number of people in the dining party.

<!-- ![From Adobe Stock](https://github.com/hungtong/DS-01-101/blob/main/image/7_tips.png?raw=true){fig-align="center" width="239"} -->

```{r}
#| eval: true

load(url('https://github.com/hungtong/DS-01-101/raw/refs/heads/main/dataset/tips.rdata'))

glimpse(tips)
```

-   The categories of `sex` and `day` are not consistent. To fix `sex`, we can do the following:

```{r}
#| eval: true

tips$sex[tips$sex %in% c('f', 'F', 'female', 'Female', 'FEMALE', 'female', 'Female', 'female ', 'Female ')] <- 'female'

tips$sex[tips$sex %in% c('m', 'M', 'male', 'Male', 'MALE', 'Male', 'Male ')] <- 'male'
```
